title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a woman called Priya Shah, who designs small city gardens.
audio_script: Interviewer: This morning I'm talking to Priya Shah, who designs gardens for people living in cities. Priya, were you always interested in plants? || Guest: I liked them, but I didn't imagine making a career from them. I studied art first, and later worked in a furniture shop. [20]It was helping customers choose plants for tiny balconies that made me realise I enjoyed solving space problems[/20]. That's how it began. || Interviewer: What do people usually want when they contact you? || Guest: Many ask for somewhere to sit outside, even if their garden is very small. Some also want vegetables. [21]The first thing I do is ask how they actually use the space, not what looks fashionable in magazines[/21]. || Interviewer: What mistake do people often make? || Guest: They buy too many plants at once because everything looks lovely in the shop. Then the garden becomes crowded. [22]I usually suggest choosing fewer plants that will grow well in the available light[/22]. That gives a calmer result. || Interviewer: What is difficult about working in city gardens? || Guest: Access can be a problem. I've carried soil through narrow flats and up staircases. But [23]the hardest thing is often noise, because people want a peaceful garden even when there is traffic nearby[/23]. You have to design carefully. || Interviewer: Do you have a favourite type of project? || Guest: I enjoy roof gardens, but they involve lots of rules. [24]My favourite projects are family gardens where children can play and adults can still have somewhere pleasant to sit[/24]. That balance is interesting. || Interviewer: How has your work changed recently? || Guest: People are thinking more about wildlife. [25]More clients now ask for flowers that attract bees and butterflies, which is a very positive change[/25]. It means gardens can be useful as well as attractive. || Interviewer: What do you do before drawing a design? || Guest: I visit at different times of day if I can. A place that seems sunny in the morning may be dark by afternoon. I also listen to sounds and look from the windows, because people often see the garden from inside more than they sit in it. || Interviewer: Could anyone improve a small garden without hiring a designer? || Guest: Definitely. Start with one corner and make it work well. A comfortable chair, one good pot and a plant that suits the light can change how a space feels. People sometimes wait until they can afford a big project, but small improvements can make them use the garden more often. || Interviewer: What is the smallest space you have worked on? || Guest: A balcony that was hardly wider than a table. The owner wanted somewhere to drink coffee in the morning, so we used folding furniture and pots on the wall rather than the floor. It became a tiny but useful space. || Interviewer: Do people need to spend a lot to make a garden pleasant? || Guest: Not always. Good planning matters more than buying expensive things. If you understand where the sun falls and choose plants that suit the place, even a few simple pots can look lovely and last for years. || Interviewer: What season do you like best for your work? || Guest: Spring, because clients suddenly notice their outdoor spaces again.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: This morning I'm talking to Priya Shah, who designs gardens for people living in cities. Priya, were you always interested in plants?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I liked them, but I didn't imagine making a career from them. I studied art first, and later worked in a furniture shop. It was helping customers choose plants for tiny balconies that made me realise I enjoyed solving space problems. That's how it began.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do people usually want when they contact you?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Many ask for somewhere to sit outside, even if their garden is very small. Some also want vegetables. The first thing I do is ask how they actually use the space, not what looks fashionable in magazines.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What mistake do people often make?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: They buy too many plants at once because everything looks lovely in the shop. Then the garden becomes crowded. I usually suggest choosing fewer plants that will grow well in the available light. That gives a calmer result.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What is difficult about working in city gardens?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Access can be a problem. I've carried soil through narrow flats and up staircases. But the hardest thing is often noise, because people want a peaceful garden even when there is traffic nearby. You have to design carefully.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you have a favourite type of project?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I enjoy roof gardens, but they involve lots of rules. My favourite projects are family gardens where children can play and adults can still have somewhere pleasant to sit. That balance is interesting.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: How has your work changed recently?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: People are thinking more about wildlife. More clients now ask for flowers that attract bees and butterflies, which is a very positive change. It means gardens can be useful as well as attractive.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do you do before drawing a design?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I visit at different times of day if I can. A place that seems sunny in the morning may be dark by afternoon. I also listen to sounds and look from the windows, because people often see the garden from inside more than they sit in it.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Could anyone improve a small garden without hiring a designer?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Definitely. Start with one corner and make it work well. A comfortable chair, one good pot and a plant that suits the light can change how a space feels. People sometimes wait until they can afford a big project, but small improvements can make them use the garden more often.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What is the smallest space you have worked on?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: A balcony that was hardly wider than a table. The owner wanted somewhere to drink coffee in the morning, so we used folding furniture and pots on the wall rather than the floor. It became a tiny but useful space.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do people need to spend a lot to make a garden pleasant?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Not always. Good planning matters more than buying expensive things. If you understand where the sun falls and choose plants that suit the place, even a few simple pots can look lovely and last for years.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What season do you like best for your work?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Spring, because clients suddenly notice their outdoor spaces again.

questions.question: How did Priya become interested in designing gardens?

options.B: She helped her family with their garden.
options.C: She enjoyed finding plant solutions for small spaces.

questions.explanation: Priya says helping customers choose plants for tiny balconies made her enjoy solving space problems.

questions.question: What does Priya do first with new clients?

options.A: She asks about the way they use the garden.
options.B: She shows them fashionable garden pictures.
options.C: She checks how much money they can spend.

questions.explanation: She says she first asks how people actually use the space.

questions.question: What advice does Priya often give about plants?

options.A: Buy plants at different times of year.
options.B: Choose fewer plants that suit the light.
options.C: Use the same plants as neighbours.

questions.explanation: She suggests choosing fewer plants that will grow well in the available light.

questions.question: What is often the biggest challenge in city gardens?
questions.explanation: Priya says the hardest thing is often noise, because people want a peaceful garden.

questions.question: What kind of project does Priya like best?

options.A: A garden that works for both children and adults.
options.B: A roof garden with unusual rules.
options.C: A vegetable garden for a whole family.

questions.explanation: She says her favourite projects are family gardens where children can play and adults can sit.

questions.question: What recent change does Priya mention?

options.B: Clients are more interested in helping wildlife.

questions.explanation: She says more clients ask for flowers that attract bees and butterflies.
